data.title: Bumble: Fundamentals Are Shaky Amid 2026 Product Reset
data.description:
While the stock market has quickly rebounded to fresh all-time highs, most small and mid-cap tech stocks are suffering at multi-year lows, thanks to the rise of the "SaaSpocalypse" narrative.

There are few exceptions to this deep rout amid small-cap growth, and Bumble (BMBL) surprisingly is one of them. The women-first dating app has been undergoing a rocky period as paying users defect and founder Whitney Wolfe Herd returned as CEO in March 2025. Though Bumble continues to lose paid users, the market is having increased confidence in a product turnaround, driving Bumble up ~20% year to date. The question for investors now: Is this a phantom rally, or is Bumble truly on a rebound kick?

I last wrote about a "Sell" rating on Bumble in November, when the stock was trading at $3 per share. I'll admit to being impressed at many of the actions Bumble is taking to right-size its business, including strengthening its core brand identity, raising profile standards, and setting the stage for more AI-driven matchmaking. At the same time, it's difficult for me to be bullish on the future when Bumble has seen an incessant decline in paid users. Until we see evidence that Bumble's product initiatives are leading to stabilization in revenue, this stock is uncomfortable as a long hold. As such, I reiterate my "Sell" rating on this stock.

The next major catalyst for Bumble is its Q1 earnings release, set for May 5. In my view, trends are moving in the wrong direction for Bumble, especially as major competitor Match (MTCH) steps up its own rebound strategy for its core product, Tinder. We think tougher competitive dynamics will make it tough for Bumble to succeed in its revitalization, in both Q1 and throughout FY26.

Bumble's user trends are looking worse against Hinge

By now, we've been bombarded with many headlines of dating app fatigue. This is clearly evidenced by a loss of paid users in Bumble and Match, as well as the rise of "IRL" social gatherings like run clubs. Increasingly, companies like Bumble and Match are trying to leverage AI as a hook to attract new users, promising more AI-driven matchmaking that can drive better matches and make easier the process of creating a profile and organizing dates.

To me, this increasingly sounds like the dating app industry is becoming a slower-growth, zero-sum game: where the total pool of paid users is no longer expanding and may even be contracting.

This is an unfavorable setup for Bumble in 2026, especially as it continues a multi-quarter trend of steep paid user declines. In Bumble's fourth-quarter update (which the company released recently in mid-March), the company showcased a 22% y/y decline in paid users to 2.185 million on the core Bumble app. It shed 159k paid users in Q4, which is similar to the 156k paid users that it lost in Q3.

And it's not just the Bumble app, either. The company's Badoo app, which is just shy of 20% of overall revenue, also lost 95k paid users, doubling versus a 47k loss in Q3.

Now, to be clear, this is an industry problem, not just limited to Bumble's execution. In Q4, Tinder also lost 489k paid users to end at 8.77 million payers, or -8% y/y.

And yet we note two things: Tinder's y/y decline at 8% y/y is far more benign than the core Bumble app, which is down 22% y/y. Secondly, Match has the benefit of the rising popularity of Hinge, which added 13k paid users in Q4 and grew 16% y/y.

Bumble and Match have different advertising strategies entering into 2026

Now that we've established that Bumble is on slightly weaker footing versus Tinder, and substantially weaker footing than Hinge, we now turn the discussion to why Bumble's performance will wither even further in 2026: the two companies' marketing budget differences are widening as well.

Bumble's brand reset involves a return of focus on telling the core Bumble brand story, which emphasizes healthier dating app interactions and empowering women to make the first move. As such, for 2026 and beyond, the company is focused on organic user acquisitions and letting its improved product drive registrations. At the same time, the company is deeply slashing its performance marketing budget. Per Whitney Wolfe Herd's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

We began by raising standards across the platform and fundamentally changing how we approach acquisitions. Performance marketing was reduced by well over 80% year-over-year.

That was a deliberate shift away from volume-based acquisition and towards higher intent, organically driven growth as we return to our roots of brand and organic marketing. Despite raising the bar on new members and dramatically limiting marketing, Bumble app registrations and active users have stabilized. We proved 2 things through this exercise: first, demand for what we offer, the promise of human connection, relationships and potentially look is strong and enduring. Second, we believe the Bumble brand has tremendous affinity with our target audience as we tighten trust and authenticity standards, user metrics behaved as expected.

But whereas Bumble is pulling back on down-funnel performance marketing, Match is focused on driving a Tinder rebound through increasing down-funnel advertising, rather than relying on organic traffic and brand marketing. Per CEO Spencer Rascoff's remarks on the Match Q4 earnings call:

Regarding Prism, Prism for those that don't know, is our first ever Match Group-wide analysis of measuring marketing efficacy across brands. So we've been able to put every single penny we spend on advertising across every single brand on an apples-to-apples basis and look at the efficacy of ad spend.

What it exposed to us was the benefits for brands like Tinder to be more focused on down-funnel advertising performance and user acquisition advertising rather than top-of-funnel brand advertising. And it informed the increased spend on Tinder from $180 million to $230 million. And it also unlocked a lot of other learnings in the E&E brands as well as the Hinge brand.

Put in short: Bumble and Match are adopting widely different playbooks for how to reclaim users in a tough dating app environment. And while the jury is still out, I expect this will cause an even wider divergence between Bumble and Match's paid user trends in 2026. Already, Bumble exited Q4 at much steeper y/y payer losses than Match; with a thinner war chest for advertising, Bumble's product revamp story may be outshined by Tinder.

Upside risks and key takeaways

Of course, we should be aware of the upside risks in Bumble as well. Trends are unpredictable on social media. Bumble's shift away from performance-based marketing and more towards brand identity may be read as more authentic than Tinder, which appears to be more aggressive in targeting new users.

Also, we note that Bumble trades at a significant valuation discount to Match, with its ~4x forward adjusted EBITDA multiple sitting at ~half of Match's valuation. This may drive more value buyers into the stock.

That said, overall we think the setup for Bumble is unfavorable as we head into the Q1 earnings season. "Sell" this stock and invest elsewhere.

data.title: Netflix Q1: Murky Forward Subscriber Outlook (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
With the Warner play in the rearview, investors were likely waiting for Netflix, Inc. (NFLX), to show why it’s better off as a standalone streaming giant. That validation didn’t occur in Q1, and investors appear to be responding accordingly.

In February, I wrote a bullish piece on NFLX. In my publication, I expressed bullishness on the Warner deal and elaborated on why getting it done was so important to the company. The stock had gained about 20% since that write-up.

After walking away from the potential deal, I do feel differently about NFLX, though I still view the forward outlook as positive.

In the immediate period ahead, it’s possible that NFLX rallies higher along with the broader markets. I believe that investors could likely recover the roughly 10% loss in NFLX’s stock since its Q1 release.

However, keeping with the selectivity theme that I laid out in my 2026 market outlook article that was published at the end of 2025, I believe prospective investors in NFLX could benefit from waiting for the dust to settle before initiating new positioning.

One question that I ask is whether NFLX believes internally that subscriber growth could weaken. After all, NFLX did leave the forward forecast unchanged, despite the recent price hike in its subscription plan.

Since NFLX no longer reports subscription stats, this will be difficult to ascertain, but I imagine it will be brought up in any future earnings discussion. Confirmation that the subscription base is growing and not weakening would be the affirmation I need to return to a more bullish posture on the stock.

Why Did NFLX Stock Decline After Its Q1 Results?

At the surface level, this looked like a good quarter for NFLX. Both revenues and earnings came in ahead of expectations, while margins improved YOY. And yet, shares fell nearly 10%.

I believe the decline, therefore, had very little to do with what NFLX did in Q1 and almost everything to do with what NFLX forecasted for the periods ahead. In my view, the issue for NFLX was more about what wasn’t said than what was.

NFLX guided Q2 below Street expectations and left its full-year forecast unchanged just weeks after raising U.S. subscription prices. I believe that double whammy raised an uncomfortable question for investors. If pricing is higher, why isn’t the full-year outlook moving higher too?

Because NFLX no longer reports quarterly subscriber figures, investors don’t have a clean way to verify whether engagement and net adds remain strong or whether pricing could be creating friction. In my view, the unchanged outlook may suggest the management team may be bracing for softer subscriber momentum, even if they aren’t saying so outright.

Add onto that the departure of founder Reed Hastings from the board, lingering questions about NFLX’s post-Warner standalone strategy, and a stock that continues to command a premium valuation that doesn’t really leave it room for any missteps, and it becomes clearer as to why the market reacted as it did.

NFLX Q1 Results Recap

I believe that NFLX actually delivered a very strong Q1 by almost any traditional metric.

Revenue, for example, grew 16% YOY. Operating income, meanwhile, rose 18%. Both landed ahead of guidance due to better-than-expected subscription revenue.

Furthermore, operating margin came in at 32.3%, above last year’s 31.7%, while diluted EPS surged 86%. This was helped in part by the +$2.8B termination fee tied to the Warner transaction.

Overall engagement also appears to be trending in the right direction. On the presser, NFLX mentioned that its primary internal quality engagement metric reached an all-time high during the quarter, supported by major releases, live events like the World Baseball Classic in Japan, and expansion into video podcasts and kids gaming.

Advertising momentum also appears to be holding up well, with the ad tier representing over 60% of sign-ups in ad markets and ad revenue still tracking toward ~+$3B this year.

Cash flow was also extremely strong, in my opinion. Free cash flow ("FCF") was +$5.1B in the quarter versus $2.7B a year ago. While the forward guidance was unchanged overall, the lift in full-year FCF expectations to +$12.5B is a definite positive in my lens.

By the numbers, this was a quarter that showed operational strength, improving monetization, and rising engagement. In my view, this makes the market’s negative reaction feel even more tied to forward expectations than anything that actually happened in Q1.

What Is The Outlook For NFLX Stock?

The forward outlook is where the sun begins setting for NFLX, in my view.

For Q2, NFLX guided to 13% revenue growth and an operating margin of 32.6%, down from 34.1% in the year-ago quarter. Both revenues and earnings were below consensus estimates.

For its part, NFLX pointed this to content amortization being heavily weighted toward the first half of the year, with margin expansion expected to reaccelerate in Q3 and Q4 to reach the full-year target.

For the full year, NFLX reiterated its prior forecast. Importantly, this guidance was left unchanged despite recent U.S. price increases and a strong Q1.

The issue, I think, for investors isn’t that the outlook is weak. It’s that it didn’t improve. After a price hike, strong Q1 metrics, and added advertising momentum, I believe the unchanged full-year guide leaves open the question of whether subscriber growth could be moderating beneath the surface.

Looking further out, NFLX is continuing to emphasize three pillars. This includes driving higher engagement through broader entertainment formats, leveraging AI and product improvements to enhance the member experience, and improving monetization through pricing power and a rapidly scaling ads business expected to reach ~+$3B this year. I believe all three are attainable and remain positive factors for the forward outlook.

Is NFLX Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

Despite the market’s overall negative reaction to Q1, it’s worth noting that several analysts came out quickly defending the quarter.

In looking at those ‘buy’ calls, I believe the common thread in those notes was that expectations, particularly around pricing flowing through and near-term margins, were likely too optimistic, not that the business itself is weakening. Many pointed to NFLX’s pricing power, engagement strength, and long runway for monetization as reasons to view the pullback as a buying opportunity.

I understand that view. In fact, if you look strictly at engagement trends, advertising momentum, margin expansion, and FCF generation, the bull case remains very intact.

My hesitation, however, is centered on what we can’t see.

Because NFLX no longer discloses subscriber figures, I believe that investors are being asked to take NFLX's word that pricing, engagement, and retention remain healthy, even as the full-year outlook stays flat following a price hike. For me, that creates just enough uncertainty to step back from my previous ‘buy’ rating.

I’m not bearish on NFLX. I’m simply waiting for confirmation that subscriber momentum remains strong in this post-Warner, higher-price environment before getting bullish again. For now, I view NFLX as a 'hold.'

data.title: Eli Lilly: From Sell To Buy In 90 Days, Here's What Changed My Mind
data.description:
Introduction

Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) has dropped over 13% since I last rated it a Sell back in January, and with the firm’s incoming quarter as well as the just-announced deal for Kelonia, I want to reassess if my rating still holds. Furthermore, I am still a shareholder in Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO), so I would like to see which one of the firms is still in the lead in the weight loss industry.

The Kelonia Therapeutics deal

Eli Lilly was initially reported by the Wall Street Journal to be in advanced negotiations to acquire Kelonia Therapeutics for more than $2B, and the deal just closed for an amount that could be worth up to $7B in cash. This is a major stepping stone for Kelonia, as it was last publicly valued at slightly more than $100MM in April 2022. In all, $3.25B will be paid upfront and the rest based on milestones. But what’s more impressive would be the actual tech from the firm, which is the IGPS platform using modified lentiviral vectors to deliver CAR constructs directly into a patient’s T cells inside the body. This is potentially a major milestone, as it could erase the need for lymphodepleting chemotherapy.

The lead candidate, KLN-1010, is an in vivo anti-BCMA CAR-T therapy for relapsed and refractory multiple myeloma currently in Phase 1. The data presented at ASH 2025 in December showed a 100% MRD-negative response rate across the first four patients with no Grade 3+ cytokine release syndrome and no ICANs. This clean safety profile is essentially the gatekeeper for moving CAR-T from late-line salvage therapy to earlier-line and potentially frontline, community-based treatment.

On top of Kelonia, LLY also acquired Orna Therapeutics for up to $2.4B in February in yet another bet on in vivo CAR-T therapy, but this time for autoimmune conditions. Basically, Lilly is building a diversified in vivo cell therapy portfolio, delivering a platform to build on. Rivals using ex vivo CAR-T therapies, such as J&J/Legend's Carvykti, which generated $1.9B in 2025 sales, require complex manufacturing as well as chemotherapy conditioning and weeks of turnaround time. But the in vivo CAR-T sidesteps this entire manufacturing bottleneck.

On the macro side, the market for in vivo CAR-T is set to grow from $368MM in 2024 to $44B by 2034, representing an astonishing 61.5% CAGR. This would simply be an unprecedented growth rate, which is an unsure projection, but the direction is certainly clear as the industry is moving toward off-the-shelf and injectable cell therapies that reduce the logistical and cost barriers for the treatments currently available on the market.

Eli Lilly Q1 Earnings—What to Watch for in the Incoming Quarter

First, I’ll look at the current dynamic for Mounjaro & Zepbound volume vs. price. As in the latest quarter, Mounjaro generated $7.4B and Zepbound $4.3B in overall sales, resulting in $11.7B in revenue for a single quarter. I want to look at what the real net pricing for these will be, as it has already declined 7% in the US for the past quarter, and if the volume acceleration can effectively offset this. The consensus is looking for around $17.6B in total revenue, which would effectively imply a sequential reduction. So the overall question would be, is LLY gaining units faster than it is losing price? The combination of the White House deal, the Medicaid dynamics, and the China reimbursement list inclusion means that there will still be pressure on net realized prices.

Secondly, Foundayo, or orforglipron, was just approved on April 1 and started shipping via LillyDirect on April 6th at $149/month cash-pay and $25 per month with commercial coverage. Though that gives commercial availability before the quarter closes but will certainly not really impact Q1 revenue, it will be enough for management to provide a glimpse into the starting prescription trends and payer coverage decisions. It will be crucial to evaluate it against Novo’s Wegovy pill, which launched in January and reached roughly 3,100 patients in the first week. If Foundayo can reach that level, that will set the tone heading into Q2.

Other than that, the FDA approval letter for Foundayo included a request for postmarketing clinical trial data on MACE, delayed gastric emptying pressure, and drug-induced liver injury. This created a brief selling pressure, but LLY released the ACHIEVE-4 topline results on April 16, and the data looks relatively clean with no DILI signal detected across more than 2,700 patients. The trial also showed non-inferior cardiovascular safety versus insulin glargine with a 16% lower observed MACE rate and an exploratory 57% lower all-cause mortality.

And finally, during Q4, LLY guided for up to $83B in revenue and $35 in non-GAAP EPS for the full year 2026. This would imply a roughly 25% topline growth at the midpoint, while Novo guided for a decline in its topline. That’s why both companies were headed in a drastically different direction post-earnings release. I’ll closely watch both to see if there is any amendment to their guidance to better reflect the tariff and pricing environment.

LLY stock valuation

My biggest bearish take has always been LLY’s valuation, and though it has depreciated a bit, LLY's P/E GAAP is still hovering at over 40, nearly 4 times the level of Novo Nordisk and about twice the multiple for Pfizer Inc. (PFE) and Novartis AG (NVS). On a free cash flow basis, the firm is only yielding 1.1% at current prices, while Novo is at a strong 5.1%. I also did a DCF and a scenario analysis on the company to better grasp the valuation thesis. On a DCF basis, if the company can keep this relentless rate of growth, it would be possible for the firm to have an implied share price of $1,575, a nearly 70% upside. But that would mean that the firm continues to grow at over 10% by 2030. In a scenario-based analysis, the implied share price drops to $1,064 on a probability-weighted fair value.

Risks

Lilly’s financial performance is heavily tilted toward its metabolic franchise, which now comprises around 56% of the company’s total revenue. This creates a high degree of vulnerability to pricing headwinds such as the most favored nation pricing deal, as well as the new TrumpRx platform, on which Lilly is offering high discounts that the CFO mentioned would act as a low-to-mid-teens percentage drag on top-line growth in 2026.

Conclusion

I’m bullish on the overall weight loss market, with a preference for Novo Nordisk, as it also offers a clear undervaluation thesis considering its rock-bottom valuation metrics. But I have to say that Q4 changed my stance on Eli Lilly, and I’m considering buying into the company thanks to its latest drawdown. It is expensive, but if it can sustain this level of growth compared to Novo, it might be a clear winner for your portfolio. In a sense, I would recommend buying both companies. I upgrade my rating to a Buy, and I will buy shares post-earnings if there is a clear indication that it can grow by over 25% this year.

data.title: How A Steeper Yield Curve Could Completely Reshape Dividend Investing
data.description:
Recently, the Federal Reserve has been cutting interest rates while at the same time the 10-year Treasury yield has remained range-bound. This has resulted in the yield curve getting steeper, which has been a big disappointment to many investors who were counting on rate cuts leading to lower long-term interest rates, and has led to lingering underperformance in bond proxies like real estate investment trusts (VNQ), such as Realty Income (O). This combination of a sticky long end of the curve, a Federal Reserve that has generally been in an easing mode, and ultimately a steepening yield curve has significant implications for dividend investing. In this article, I will detail several of the most important ones.

What The CME FedWatch Tool Is Telling Us

The CME FedWatch tool currently puts:

  • 56.5% odds that the Fed stands pat with no rate cuts or rate hikes this year.

  • A 0.3% chance that the Fed hikes by 25 basis points.

  • A 34.3% chance that the Fed cuts by 25 basis points.

  • An 8% chance that the Fed cuts by 50 basis points.

  • A 0.9% chance that the Fed cuts by 75 basis points by year's end.

What this indicates is that the most likely scenario is that the Fed does nothing, but there is also a meaningful chance that the Fed cuts by at least 25 basis points by year's end. Meanwhile, the long end of the curve has been kept in a pretty stable range due to persistent federal deficits leading to heavy Treasury issuance of bonds and lingering concerns about inflation, as well as ongoing global de-dollarization, especially with major central banks and other institutions shedding U.S. Treasuries in favor of alternatives like gold.

The Chart That Changes Everything

This dynamic is evident in the chart below, which shows that as the Fed has cut rates fairly aggressively from 5.5% to 3.75% over the past few years, the 10-year Treasury rate has remained quite stable over that period.

This is not entirely surprising, given that the yield curve was previously inverted and has only recently become uninverted, but it is still a disappointment to many investors who were hoping for lower long-term interest rates to follow Federal Reserve rate cuts. It is also worth noting that the 10-2 year Treasury yield spread still has a long way to run before it approaches recent highs over the past decade. This means that the yield curve could very likely steepen significantly more in the coming months.

This means that investors who are banking on long-term interest rates dropping in the near future are likely going to have to be quite patient, unless the timeline for Federal Reserve rate cuts becomes much more accelerated than is currently priced in. What this all means is that investors need to expect a higher-for-longer interest rate environment at the long end of the curve.

The Brutal Truth For REITs And Utilities

In particular, this will have a generally neutral to negative impact on the REIT sector, since REITs are generally quite interest-rate sensitive and viewed as bond proxies. If long-term interest rates do not move materially lower, there will not be a large tailwind pushing REIT valuations higher. Generally, REITs benefit from lower interest rates, since it moves their cap rates lower, their NAVs higher, the attractiveness of public equities higher, and interest costs lower.

That does not mean that the sector is to be completely avoided, though, because there are plenty of REITs that are attractively valued that can still see valuation multiple expansion. It simply means that investors should not be expecting to receive an extra tailwind and that the bet should not be on interest rates falling but rather on current yield plus growth potential being sufficient to drive total returns, with the potential valuation multiple bump simply from the market sentiment improving on that individual business rather than the whole sector shooting higher due to falling interest rates.

As far as utilities (XLU) go, they are likely going to have a similar interest rate dynamic as REITs, as they are often viewed as bond proxies, often have a considerable amount of long-term fixed-rate debt on the liability side of their balance sheets, and tend to see their stock prices soar whenever interest rates fall. This means that they likely will not be getting any extra boosts, so investors should continue to be quite picky in terms of valuation fundamentals and only buy utilities that they expect to deliver attractive yield plus growth, rather than leaning on valuation multiple expansion tailwinds from falling long-term interest rates as part of the investment thesis.

The Bright Spots: BDCs And Midstream

For business development companies (BIZD), the outlook should be improving, as the lower chance of Federal Reserve rate cuts in the near future means that they will see fewer income headwinds. This is due to the fact that the vast majority of their portfolios tend to be floating-rate loans that are indexed to short-term rates, while long-term interest rates only have a small impact on their debt costs, since many of them have a large percentage of their debt indexed to floating short-term interest rates as well.

Midstream energy (AMLP) should be able to hold up just fine in the current environment because, between inflation-linked contract escalators and general benefits to energy when inflation is elevated, these companies are not nearly as beholden to interest rates as utilities and REITs are and should therefore be relatively unimpacted by this.

As for the broader dividend growth universe seen in funds like Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) or even in the dividend aristocrats (NOBL) and dividend kings, generally speaking, this is not great news, as higher-for-longer interest rates are generally a headwind for them. However, if you pick individual names that have strong balance sheets with limited need to refinance large amounts of debt and that have strong underlying growth potential in the businesses they operate, you can do just fine here as well.

Why A Yield-Plus-Growth Focus Could Save Your Portfolio

How I am approaching all this is to continue to overweight the midstream space while also selectively buying REITs, BDCs, and infrastructure opportunities. However, in the REIT, BDC, and infrastructure spaces, I'm being highly selective and insisting on large margins of safety where these securities offer attractive combinations of yield and growth and also trade at meaningful discounts to their underlying private market value, while also having very strong balance sheets. This way, I'm relatively insulated from a higher-for-longer long-term interest rate environment, can see significant valuation multiple expansion without needing interest rates to drop meaningfully, and have an attractive intrinsic path to outsize total returns simply from yield and growth.

While it is always possible that the Fed will pivot towards rate cuts this year if the war in Iran ends sooner rather than later and inflation drops and/or the disruptions to the global supply chain and elevated energy costs stemming from the war in Iran push the economy towards recession, which could also cause the Fed to cut rates more aggressively to try to prop up the economy, especially in an election year, there is also a real risk that long-term interest rates will move higher if inflation does indeed move higher and stay higher for longer than expected, especially if the war in Iran becomes prolonged and energy costs stay high for an extended period.

The One Strategy That Works No Matter What Rates Do

Before closing, I think it is important to emphasize that it is particularly important to have a diversified, balanced portfolio right now and to focus more than ever on the fundamentals and the yield-plus-growth story rather than trying to make bets on directional material changes in the interest rate, because I think that we are likely going to see a range-bound short-term and long-term interest rate level for the foreseeable future. By focusing on valuations and fundamentals, along with an opportunistic capital recycling program, I believe that my 7-8% yielding, total return outperforming, and below-market beta portfolios at High Yield Investor are positioned to continue performing well even without any help from falling interest rates in the near future.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Prosus: Suffering From Its Own Success
data.description:
Prosus N.V. (PROSY) has been trying to find its identity recently by putting all of its holdings under its umbrella on the pedestal to lose the discount that made it such a massive company in the first place. It is suffering from its success, and it needs to actively do something about it in order for investors to see it as anything else.

The Tencent Discount

In short summary, Naspers Limited (NPSNY), which made PROSY and owns 43% of it right now, first invested in Tencent Holdings Limited (TCEHY) in 2001, then a small Internet company. It invested $32m. It was the best investment decision I made because of how big Tencent has gotten since then. The original $32 million had turned into $175 billion at one point.

As of the latest half-year report, Prosus still owns a 23% stake in Tencent, which means that, at the current market value of $588B, the Prosus stake in TCEHY is worth $135.2B. This is already more than its current market cap of $121.5B. Ever since the company made this amazing investment in Tencent, which has grown exponentially over time, it cannot shake the title of being a proxy for Tencent in Europe. Currently, it remains true. What appears to be quite attractive is that if you want to own Tencent, you can buy Prosus, essentially buying Tencent at a discount of around 11%. And what does that mean? All other companies under the Prosus umbrella are being valued at a negative. They are also worth billions of dollars, yet it doesn’t seem to matter one bit.

One can argue that Tencent is already trading at a decent discount just by being a Chinese firm, which carries a lot of geopolitical risks and other risks that apply to all Chinese companies, which is mainly the fact that the Chinese government can interfere at any moment and disrupt the operations of Tencent, no matter if it is one of the biggest Chinese companies. If TCEHY does something that the government doesn’t like, they will certainly not hesitate to voice their dislike.

So, what are you getting if you do want to buy Tencent through a proxy like Prosus? You are getting a massive gaming library that has been growing at a very respectable rate, especially internationally, with Supercell, PUBG Mobile, and Wuthering Waves, as well as domestically, with games like Honor of Kings, Valorant, and Delta Force. Social networks like WeChat, which is the only true super-app in the world, have a massive user base of 1.4B monthly active users. The super app is a messaging app, similar to WhatsApp; it is a social media platform, like Instagram and Facebook; it is also a payment network, processing trillions of dollars annually.

That is not all. It is also an app store, with millions of mini-programs running within WeChat, with no downloads required, a search engine, a video platform, an e-commerce marketplace, and finally, an enterprise SaaS platform. And you may think this is too much for one app, but the fact is that all of these functions are deeply integrated so that the social graph powers the payment graph, which powers advertising, which goes on to power the commerce part of things.

This is a very unique application, with no equivalent of it in the Western world. Meta has the messaging and social media networks, but they are still all separate. You cannot buy this exposure anywhere else in Europe or the U.S., other than TCEHY itself.

Another exposure you get is in the world’s largest gaming company, which includes private assets. Riot Games, owned 100% by TCEHY, is the maker of very popular games like Valorant, the ever-popular League of Legends, and Teamfight Tactics. There is also Supercell, which is also majority owned by Tencent. They make very addictive mobile games, such as Clash of Clans, Clash Royale, and Brawl Stars. Epic Games, which has been sitting on an absolute cash cow that is Fortnite, has Tencent owning around 28% stake in the company, right after the CEO, Tim Sweeney, who owns 41%. Unreal Engine continues to be the main engine that most developers like to showcase their games. So, this is the only way you get this much exposure to Epic Games. Another way would be to own The Walt Disney Company (DIS), but the exposure would be only 9%.

Another massive opportunity here is with AI. Shocking, I know. With Tencent’s massive user base through WeChat, Yuanbao AI agent's daily active users exceeded 50 million and 114 million monthly active users. Furthermore, back in March, the company rolled out a new feature for WeChat that integrates the new OpenClaw agent, ClawBot, which will expand its AI strategy considerably. Tencent’s advertising revenues were driven by AI as well. In the latest quarter, Q4 ’25, revenue increased 17% y/y, helping gross margin expansion. Tencent Cloud and other AI services also saw amazing results recently. As of fiscal 2025 reporting, TCEHY’s revenues from B2B increased 22%, as AI is becoming a real growth driver.

So, you get AI and cloud exposure, which is the biggest growth driver currently. AI growth is the only growth driver that is keeping the broad indices propped up right now. And the good thing about TCEHY is that because of the discount due to China, you are getting AI exposure at much cheaper multiples. TCEHY currently trades at around 14x forward P/E, while the hyperscalers trade at an average of 27x forward P/E ratio (META, AMZN, MSFT, and GOOG).

The narrative shift

It does seem like Prosus is suffering from its own massive success when it invested heavily in Tencent all those years ago. We can see that the company doesn’t want to be just a proxy for Tencent in Europe. It is pushing the companies it has in its portfolio and essentially is saying, “Look at us, we are not just a 23% Tencent company; we have so much more to offer.”

One of the first indications I found is the fact that the company appointed a new CEO, Fabricio Bloisi, a Brazilian founder-operator. He built iFood and Movile into major Latin American tech businesses. iFood became Latin America’s leading and most profitable food-delivery platform. His background is a lot more operational, so it was quite unusual to see his appointment at first because Prosus looked a lot more like a holding company. So, his appointment definitely tells us that the narrative is changing.

He is aiming to take a page out of Tencent’s book and apply it to the Latin American market, as well as Europe. With food-delivery platforms like iFood and Just Eat, pair them with iFood Pago and PayU, which are the payment systems, and then add in travel experiences through the Despegar acquisition and even classifieds through OLX to get the cross-sell and data flywheel start rolling.

It was hard to see this working before, but now that most of the holdings in the portfolio have reached an inflection point in 2025, where a lot of the holdings became aEBIT-profitable, as well as free cash flow turning positive, too, it is a lot more appealing. The NAV discount should not apply to most of these companies that it has under its umbrella.

The next frontier the new CEO sees is India. He believes that the company’s investments in India will turn into a massive opportunity in the long run, akin to what the company made with the investment in Tencent all those years ago. Prosus investments in the region are quite large in terms of different investments, and back in June of last year, the CEO said that the investments in India were around $10B, and he believes that these investments will turn into a $100 billion opportunity. He even said it could be half a trillion. It’s great to hear, and expected to hear from the CEO, to essentially pump optimism in Prosus to distance itself away from the title of Tencent proxy, but what will matter the most is the execution.

Recently, Prosus had to sell its 4.5% stake in Delivery Hero to Uber Technologies, Inc. (UBER). I said it had to because that was one of the conditions of the European Commission to approve its €4.1B acquisition of Just Eat Takeaway. So, the company is getting a decent cash injection and still has around a 21% stake in Delivery Hero SE (DLVHF). The cash can be used for other activities like expanding reach in India, or somewhere else, or it can be used to continue to sell TCEHY shares aggressively into the open market to get rid of the overhang completely. This will take time, but if Prosus continues at the current pace of selling TCEHY shares in the open market while TCEHY slows down its share buybacks, we will see Prosus's stake in TCEHY falling below 20% in the near future.

Verdict: Hold

I can see it continuing to trade similarly to Tencent going forward. If TCEHY falls, PROSY will fall too. It’s inevitable for now because its current market cap is lower than its position in TCEHY. I don’t see a problem owning PROSY right now, especially since it is actively trying to shift the narrative to being a lot more than just a proxy. Even as a proxy, it is not a bad company to own, especially if you want to get some exposure to gaming and you really like what Epic Games is doing these days, or Riot.

I’m not going to initiate a position at the moment, but once the operations become a lot less convoluted, once the company’s other holdings take center stage, I will revisit Prosus' operations to see if there is something there.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Academy Sports: Valuation Discount And The 2026 World Cup
data.description:
Introduction

Unlike premium peers focused purely on elite athletics, Academy Sports and Outdoors (ASO) uses a local, "Everyday" model that protects it from e-commerce threats and appeals to middle-income shoppers. The 2026 FIFA World Cup matches are majorly concentrated in ASO’s Southern U.S. stronghold, providing a massive, priceless catalyst for sports item spending starting in June. Currently trading at a cheap price (8.7x P/E), a conservative Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model proves the market is incorrectly pricing ASO for negative growth, revealing a fair value of $85 per share.

Wall Street is currently treating Academy Sports + Outdoors (ASO) with maximum vigilance. Investors unloaded the stock because the recent Q4 earnings report moderately missed the expectations. However, looking closely at the data reveals that the miss was driven by temporary January winter storms, not an obsolete business model.

My "Strong Buy" thesis is simple. The market is looking backward at a snowy quarter and ignoring two major factors: an incredibly healthy cash flow valuation and the largest retail sports catalyst of the decade. As the 2026 FIFA World Cup approaches, ASO is strategically positioned geographically and competitively to capture massive upside that is entirely unquoted at its current low earnings multiple.

Recent Financial Performance:

Unpacking the Q4 Overreaction

To understand the current valuation disconnect, we must look at the recently reported Fiscal 2025 and Q4 results. The market reacted harshly when Q4 revenue came in at $1.72 billion, missing consensus estimates of $1.76 billion and driving a 1.6% decline in comparable sales. However, a deeper dive into the operating metrics reveals this was not a structural failure but a cyclical anomaly. Management explicitly noted that extreme January winter storms forced partial or full closures of roughly half their store fleet, costing an estimated 100 basis points in comparable sales. Despite these top-line weather headwinds, ASO actually expanded its Q4 gross margin by a massive 140 basis points to 33.6% through promotional optimization and private-label strength, proving the core profitability engine remains fully intact as they launch into their 2026 store expansion strategy.

The Competitive Scene:

To understand why ASO is a fascinating buy, we must look at the competitive environment or industry rivalry. ASO competes with DICK'S Sporting Goods (DKS) and e-commerce giants like Amazon. The Wall Street is incorrectly seeing sporting goods as dying brick-and-mortar goods.

However, ASO’s qualitative growth drivers demonstrate otherwise. ASO operates on an "Everyday Low Price" model. Whereas Dick's focuses on premium, high-end athletic boutiques, ASO successfully targets the middle-income section. Besides, their merchandise system is highly regional. One cannot comfortably buy substantial items like kayaks, gun safes, and large outdoor cooking equipment on Amazon without paying extortionate shipping fees. ASO’s inventory in hunting, fishing, and outdoor living provides a viable trench that premium athletic brands simply do not have. On top of that, ASO is expanding its high-margin in-house brands, which protects its 33.6% gross profit even during price jumps.

To quantify this competitive advantage, we must compare the margin profile to its rival, DICK'S Sporting Goods. While Dick's operates firsthand, premium-priced items result in gross margins near 35%; ASO successfully defends a nearly 34% gross margin despite operating a "Value-First" model. ASO achieves this by expanding its private-label brands (like Magellan Outdoors), which offer higher profitability and protect the company from third-party vendor price hikes.

The World Cup Is a Direct Hit for ASO’s Backyard

Beyond its competitive channel, ASO has a huge, immediate catalyst. The 2026 World Cup isn't just for soccer fans; it is a billion-dollar retail event. With matches hosted in Houston, Dallas, Atlanta, and Miami, the tournament is happening exactly where ASO has its highest store numbers.

Different from premium competitors, ASO’s pricing makes it a go-to spot for the average-income fan. As regional interest in soccer barb, ASO is perfectly positioned to capture sales in branded apparel and cooking equipment. Management has explicitly noted they expect a traffic lift from these major events, yet the stock price does not reflect this upcoming sports event.

To quantify this impact, we must look at ASO's revenue structure. Apparel and Footwear contribute roughly 50% of total sales. During major global sports events, retailers historically saw a low-to-mid single-digit bump in these above-mentioned categories due to licensed merchandise and increased youth participation. Because the 2026 games are concentrated in Texas and the Southeast—ASO's undisputed territory—even a conservative 2% to 3% uplift in their Apparel/Footwear sales could infuse an additional $30M to $50M in revenue that Wall Street analysts are entirely failing to count into their 2026 projections.

Valuation: the $85-$90 intrinsic value

While ASO currently trades at a highly depressed Forward P/E of roughly 9.07x (compared to DKS at over 13.95x), a rigorous Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model reveals a mispricing. To arrive at my $85 to $90 valuation, I constructed a 5-year DCF model using the following conservative assumptions:

  • The Cash Flow: Management recently reported adjusted free cash flow of $263 million for FY25. However, acknowledging the estimated $50+ million drag from January's unmatched winter storm closures, I normalized Year 1 Free Cash Flow to $350 million.

  • Near-Term Growth Rate (Years 1-5): I projected a conservative 5.0% FCF growth rate. This directly aligns with the high end of management's 2026 sales guidance, driven by their accelerated new store rollout and the operating leverage gained from the 2026 World Cup catalyst in their core markets.

  • Discount Rate (WACC): I applied a rigid 8.5% to 9.0% WACC. With a 10-year Treasury risk-free rate hovering near 4.3% and a stock beta of roughly 0.80, this discount rate provides a heavy safety buffer against broader consumer discretionary retail risks.

  • Terminal Value: I assumed a terminal perpetuity growth rate of just 2.0%, strictly matching long-term baseline inflation, which yields a Terminal Value of approximately $6.5 billion to $7.0 billion.

Bridging to Equity Value Per Share:

Discounting the projected 5-year cash flows and Terminal Value back to the present at an 8.5% WACC yields an Enterprise Value (EV) of $6.24 billion.

To bridge from Enterprise Value to Equity Value, we look at their most recent balance sheet: we add ASO’s highly liquid $330 million in cash and subtract their $480 million in long-term debt, resulting in a total Equity Value of $6.09 billion.

Dividing this Equity Value by their 68.0 million outstanding shares results in an exact intrinsic value of $89.55 per share. Therefore, my fair value target range is established at $85.00 to $90.00. At current trading levels near $55, the market is offering investors a massive, asymmetrical 50%+ upside.

The Bear Case:

What Could Go Wrong

To remain objective, I must admit this thesis has risks. The primary threat is that the low-to-middle-income consumer is being crushed by inflation. If the economy enters a true recession, even ASO's "value" pricing won't be enough to spur spending on discretionary items like sports equipment. Furthermore, as January’s winter storms showed, their heavy concentration in the Southern U.S. makes them highly vulnerable to regional weather events (like hurricanes in the Gulf), which could wipe out a quarter's profitability.

Investor Takeaway

Academy Sports is a hidden gem operating behind a huge geographical catalyst. With strong cash flow, a localized competitive advantage that protects it from Amazon, and a mathematically vigorous valuation discount, ASO is a Strong Buy. Driven by the upcoming summer sports catalyst and the steady execution of their new store rollouts, I am initiating a near-term price target of $85 within a 6- to 12-month timeframe. Investors should position themselves now before the World Cup fever moves the stock toward its fair value.

data.title: Virtus KAR Emerging Markets Small-Cap Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Market Summary

Emerging markets small-cap equities ended the first quarter down just under 1%. The year began strongly, with the benchmark MSCI Emerging Markets Small Cap Index up more than 11% by the end of February, however, many of those gains were relinquished following the outbreak of the war in Iran. Through February (and for the whole quarter), performance was driven largely by the same factors that led markets last year—most notably AI and related industries. This was reflected geographically, with Taiwan and Korea being the strongest performing countries, and sector-wise, with information technology and energy delivering the strongest returns. Worth noting, energy was outperforming even before oil prices went up in March, which we would attribute to the AI theme. Notably, even during March’s risk-off environment, AI-related exposures continued to outperform except for Korea.

Fund Performance

The Virtus KAR Emerging Markets Small-Cap Fund returned -0.06% (Class I), slightly outperforming the -0.74% return of the MSCI Emerging Markets Small Cap Index. Fund performance initially lagged as the market rallied through February, led by AI enthusiasm, but outperformed in March as markets declined. The initial performance lag from being underexposed to AI markets (Taiwan and Korea) was offset over the balance of the quarter by contributions from our underweight exposure to these regions, as well as to India—which has been performing weakly—and from positive stock-specific results.

LEENO Industrial and Caixa Seguridade were the largest contributors to performance during the quarter.

  • > LEENO Industrial is one of the leading manufacturers of integrated circuit chip test sockets and test probe pins used in semiconductors. The stock outperformed as the company reported strong business results, showing growth that exceeded market expectations.
  • > Caixa Seguridade is one of the largest insurance brokers in Brazil. The company has attractive joint venture interests in the entities that underwrite the policies Caixa distributes. The company reported modest quarterly results, but a strong rally in Brazilian equities brought positive sentiment to share price performance.

Avia Avian (AVVN.F) and Allegro were the largest detractors from performance in the quarter.

  • > Avia Avian is a leading paint company in Indonesia. Despite a positive year-end earnings report, the company’s shares underperformed alongside other Indonesian equities after MSCI warned Indonesian authorities that it could be downgraded to “frontier market” status unless certain reforms were implemented. Regardless of the news, we still believe Avian’s business will be minimally impacted.
  • > Allegro is a leading e-commerce platform in Poland. The stock underperformed, along with other global e-commerce peers, due to investor concerns about potential longer-term competition from agentic AI technology. Additionally, a local logistical partner announced increased investments in its own e-commerce capabilities.

% Contribution: Absolute weighted contribution. To obtain the top/bottom holdings calculation methodology, call 800-243-4361.

Portfolio Changes

During the quarter, we purchased two stocks for the portfolio: Jack Technology, an industrial sewing machine company based in China, and Precision Tsugami (PTSGF), a Japanese manufacturer of high-precision machine tools. We sold Korean enterprise resource planning (ERP) software company Douzone Bizon, Poland-based HR technology platform Grupa Pracuj, Chinese online recruitment company Kanzhun (BZ), and Brazilian ERP software company TOTVS (TTVSY).

As of January 21, 2026, Class C Shares of the Fund are no longer available for purchase by new or existing shareholders.

For more insights on the equity markets and macroeconomic backdrop, read the KAR Market Review & Outlook on Virtus.com.

Index: The MSCI Emerging Markets Small Cap Index (net) is a free float-adjusted market capitalization-weighted index designed to measure small cap equity market performance in the global emerging markets. The index is calculated on a total return basis with net dividends reinvested. Notes on Risk: Equity Securities: The market price of equity securities may be adversely affected by financial market, industry, or issuer-specific events. Focus on a particular style or on small, medium, or large-sized companies may enhance that risk. Sanctions: The imposition of sanctions and other similar measures could cause a decline in the value and/or liquidity of securities issued by or tied to the sanctioned country and increase market volatility and disruption in the sanctioned country and throughout the world. Sanctions and other similar measures could limit or prevent the Fund from buying and selling securities (in the sanctioned country and other markets), significantly delay or prevent the settlement of transactions, and negatively impact the Fund's liquidity and performance. Foreign & Emerging Markets: Investing in foreign securities, especially in emerging markets, subjects the portfolio to additional risks such as increased volatility, currency fluctuations, less liquidity, and political, regulatory, economic, and market risk. Industrial Concentration: Because the portfolio is presently heavily weighted in the industrial sector, it will be impacted by that sector's performance more than a portfolio with broader sector diversification. Market Volatility: The value of the securities in the portfolio may go up or down in response to the prospects of individual companies and/or general economic conditions. Local, regional, or global events such as war or military conflict, terrorism, pandemic, or recession could impact the portfolio, including hampering the ability of the portfolio's manager(s) to invest its assets as intended.
